Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty chaotic. I'd listen to lectures and podcasts while commuting or doing chores since I learn best through audio. I tried to carve out about 19 hours a week, but it rarely happened at consistent times. With limited resources, I had to be creative about when and where I could actually focus on coursework.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied based on deadlines and whatever else was happening in my life. Some weeks I'd be super focused, other weeks I'd barely touch anything until the last minute. The anxiety of looming deadlines would kick in and suddenly I'd be cramming everything. It wasn't ideal, but somehow I made it work.
Q3: Usually it was either seeing a notification about something new posted, or that creeping anxiety reminding me I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes my peer support group would mention something in our chat and I'd log in to see what they were talking about. Other times I just needed to hear the lecture recordings again to process the material.
Q4: After a few weeks, checking the discussion boards became almost automatic for me. My peer group was really active there, and I genuinely enjoyed the interactions. It shifted from feeling like an obligation to something I actually wanted to do. The social aspect made the LMS feel less like a chore and more like a community space.
Q5: The lecture recordings were absolutely essential for me since I'm an auditory learner. I'd replay them multiple times, sometimes at different speeds. The discussion forums were also gold because hearing different perspectives helped concepts click. Peer interactions gave me that collaborative energy I needed to stay motivated despite my general tendency to drift.
Q6: I barely touched the reading materials, honestly. PDFs and textbook chapters just don't work for my brain the way audio does. I'd skim them occasionally, but I relied heavily on lectures and discussions to get the same information. It's not that they weren't valuable, they just weren't the right format for how I learn.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could actually hear or discuss the content rather than just read it silently. If it connected to real applications or sparked interesting debate with peers, I was all in. The moment something felt like busywork or repetitive reading, I'd mentally check out. Engagement for me is about connection, not just completion.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford mentally. When my anxiety was high, I'd focus only on what was required to survive. But sometimes the optional stuff was actually more interesting because there was no pressure attached. Without the stress of grades, I could engage more authentically with optional content.
Q9: Absolutely, I downloaded lecture audio files and listened to them offline constantly. I'd record myself summarizing concepts and play those back too. A lot of my studying happened on bus rides or while cooking, completely disconnected from the LMS. My notes were all in voice memos on my phone, which obviously wouldn't show up anywhere.
Q10: There were entire weeks where I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS would show almost nothing. I'd be listening to downloaded lectures repeatedly, discussing concepts with friends over coffee, or talking through problems with my peer support group in person. That invisible learning was actually some of my most productive time.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a lecture once, then listen to it maybe five or six times over the following days. Same with discussion threads—I'd screenshot interesting exchanges and revisit them later. The initial access was just the starting point; the real learning happened afterward, completely offline and invisible to any tracking system.
Q12: Mid-semester was brutal. My anxiety spiked, stress was through the roof, and I felt like I was drowning despite working hard. Financial pressures from having limited resources added to everything. There were days I just couldn't bring myself to open the LMS because it felt overwhelming. My peer support group really pulled me through those dark weeks.
Q13: Definitely. When I was exhausted or stressed, I'd log in but just stare at the screen without really processing anything. Competing responsibilities like work and family stuff meant the LMS often got pushed aside. Sometimes I'd open materials just to feel like I was doing something, but my brain wasn't actually engaged with the content.
Q14: More times than I'd like to admit. I'd have a video playing but my mind would be somewhere else entirely, worrying about bills or other stressors. I'd scroll through discussion posts without really reading them. It looked like engagement from the outside, but internally I was just going through the motions during those anxious periods.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly even though I knew they mattered. The anxiety made starting feel impossible—like this huge mountain I couldn't climb. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, then tomorrow would come with the same dread. It's ironic because avoiding them only made my anxiety worse, but in the moment, avoidance felt like survival.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd coast along, then panic-engage in the final days before something was due. The LMS would show these huge spikes of activity right before deadlines and then nothing. It's not healthy, but that pressure was often the only thing that could cut through my tendency to drift and procrastinate.
Q17: When I got good feedback, it genuinely boosted my confidence and made me want to engage more. Knowing I was on the right track helped manage my anxiety about the course. But if feedback was delayed or vague, I'd spiral a bit, wondering if I was completely off base. Quick, specific feedback was like a lifeline for my engagement.
Q18: The structure actually helped more than I expected. Having clear weekly modules gave me a framework even when I felt lost. The peer support component was huge for me—it created accountability and connection. But the structure also meant I could see exactly how behind I was, which sometimes increased my anxiety rather than helping.
Q19: The audio and video content made engagement much easier for my learning style. Discussion forums being active also helped because I could learn through conversation. What made it harder was when there were too many readings or when deadlines clustered together. My anxiety would spike when I felt like I couldn't possibly keep up with everything.
Q20: I think that describes me pretty well actually. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity. Sometimes you're just anxiously clicking around, trying to feel in control, or looking for something that makes sense. Other times you're genuinely engaged but struggling to translate that into completed work. The gap between understanding and submitting is real.
Q21: That resonates with me too, in a different way. My exam score was high even though my assignment completion was terrible. I was learning deeply through audio and peer discussions, just not always producing visible output. Some students just process and demonstrate knowledge differently than the system expects. Engagement isn't always measurable.
Q22: Logs can show patterns—when you're active, how long you spend on things, what you click on. But they can't show if you're actually thinking or just scrolling mindlessly. They miss all the offline learning, the conversations, the audio replays on your phone. They definitely can't capture the emotional experience of engaging with material while anxious or stressed.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it deeply. It's not just completing tasks—it's thinking about concepts when you're not even studying, discussing ideas with others, making connections. For me, engagement is auditory and social. It's listening, talking, processing out loud. It's rarely visible in clicks and timestamps.
Q24: The LMS couldn't show how much my peer support group carried me through this course emotionally and academically. It couldn't capture the hours of audio I listened to offline or the voice memos I made. It definitely couldn't show the anxiety I battled every time I logged in, or the resilience it took to keep going despite wanting to drift away. My engagement was real, just mostly invisible.
